items.title: State units with every entropy value
items.avoid: Reporting H = 0.693 without saying bits or nats.
items.prefer: Write H(X) = 1 bit or H(X) = ln 2 nats, and stick to one base within a calculation.
items.why: 0.693 is ambiguous (could be nats for a fair coin or a wrong bit value); units prevent silent factor-of-ln(2) errors.
items.example: Fair coin: H = 1 bit = ln 2 nats ≈ 0.693 nats—not 0.693 bits.

items.title: Normalize before applying the formula
items.avoid: Plugging raw class counts into −Σ p log p without dividing by the total.
items.prefer: Convert counts to probabilities summing to 1, or use `sklearn.metrics.entropy(counts, base=2)`.
items.why: The definition requires a PMF; unnormalized weights break both theory and library expectations.
items.example: Counts [40, 10] → p = [0.8, 0.2] → H ≈ 0.72 bits, not entropy computed on 40 and 10 directly.

items.title: Handle zero probabilities correctly
items.avoid: Calling log(0) on outcomes with p = 0 in naive loops.
items.prefer: Filter to p > 0 or use `np.where(p > 0, p * np.log2(p), 0.0)` so zero-mass outcomes contribute 0.
items.why: Only supported outcomes matter; the limit p log p → 0 keeps H finite.
items.example: One-hot PMF [1, 0, 0]: sum one term, H = 0, no log(0) errors.

items.title: Pair entropy with context for ML decisions
items.avoid: Choosing features or models using entropy alone when class imbalance or cost asymmetry matters.
items.prefer: Use information gain alongside other criteria (Gini, gain ratio, validation AUC) and check support at each node.
items.why: Entropy treats all misclassifications equally and can favor high-cardinality splits without regularization.
items.example: A user-ID feature may yield high information gain on training data but fail to generalize—validate splits, do not trust parent H drop alone.
